---
title: "Preface"
author: "Cali Pfleger"
date: today
---

# Preface {.unnumbered}

Welcome. This is my PhD dissertation, written as a *reproducible* book — every figure and every number in the prose is generated by code in the project repository, so the analysis and the writing stay in sync automatically.

The chapters look at climate variability through five complementary lenses:

- **Chapter 1** uses an isotope-enabled climate model to ask whether the Pacific Walker Circulation strengthens or weakens in response to forced climate change, and how that signal should look in oxygen-isotope records.
- **Chapter 2** asks whether large tropical volcanic eruptions reliably trigger El Niño events, or whether the ocean's pre-existing state controls the response. I combine a climate-model ensemble with coral-reef proxy records to settle the case.
- **Chapter 3** follows up Chapter 2 with a regional case study: do volcano-triggered ENSO signals appear in Pacific corals near the California coast?
- **Chapter 4** turns to marine biogeochemistry and fish-population ecology, asking how ocean chemistry varies geographically and what that means for the species that depend on it. *(Scope is still being finalized.)*
- **Chapter 5** steps outside the physical sciences to ask how global news media have talked about three climate-technology approaches — nature-based solutions, carbon-dioxide removal, and solar radiation management — over the past decade, using natural-language processing on the GDELT news corpus.

Each chapter stands on its own: its own dataset, methods, bibliography, and figures. A synthesis of shared themes appears in the concluding chapter.

## How to read this book

Chapters are organized in the order above, but you can read them independently. Figure captions, inline statistics, and citation jumps are linked throughout — hover any citation to preview the reference, click to jump to the chapter's bibliography.

Material flagged with a yellow **under review** callout has been added recently by the dissertation's nightly automation and has not yet been verified by the author. Treat those sections as drafts.

## Reproducibility

The analysis is fully open. Every `.qmd` source file contains embedded Python chunks; re-rendering the book re-runs the analysis and refreshes figures and numbers. The pipeline, bibliographies, and figure-sync tooling are described in the [repository README](https://github.com/calipfleger/dissertation/blob/main/README.md).

## Citation style

Chicago author-date is the default. Chapters targeting a specific journal use that journal's style; the override is declared in the chapter's YAML header.

## Acknowledgments

This work is supported by the Bren School of Environmental Science & Management at the University of California, Santa Barbara. Co-authors and collaborators are named in each chapter's acknowledgments. Questions and comments: [calipfleger@ucsb.edu](mailto:calipfleger@ucsb.edu).
